# UC-5.20.11 · IPv6 Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) Failures

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Beginner &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Availability, Security &middot; **Wave:** Walk &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We watch for the moment when a device tries to use a new internet address and discovers someone else already has it — like two families showing up to the same house. If this happens repeatedly, it means either addresses are being assigned twice by mistake, or someone is deliberately blocking new residents from moving in.*

---

## Description

Detects IPv6 Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) failures across the network. DAD is a mandatory IPv6 mechanism (RFC 4862) — before any IPv6 address can be used (SLAAC or DHCPv6-assigned), the host sends a Neighbor Solicitation to verify the address is unique on the link. If another device responds, the address is marked TENTATIVE and cannot be used, which means the host has no usable IPv6 address on that interface. A single DAD failure is an address conflict (operational issue). Multiple DAD failures in rapid succession on the same VLAN indicate either a systematic misconfiguration (overlapping address pools) or an active DAD denial-of-service attack (UC-5.20.87) where an attacker responds to ALL DAD probes, preventing any new host from acquiring an IPv6 address.

## Value

A DAD failure silently prevents a device from communicating via IPv6. Unlike IPv4 where duplicate IP addresses produce visible ARP conflicts and gratuitous ARPs, IPv6 DAD failures often go unnoticed — the device simply has no working IPv6 address and falls back to IPv4 (if dual-stack) or has no connectivity at all (if IPv6-only). Users may not report the issue because their browser uses Happy Eyeballs (RFC 8305) to transparently fall back to IPv4, masking the IPv6 failure. This UC surfaces DAD failures that would otherwise be invisible, enabling proactive remediation of address conflicts and early detection of DAD-based denial-of-service attacks.

## Implementation

Monitor syslog from all Layer 3 devices and endpoints for DAD failure messages. The search is simple — DAD failures are explicit syslog events with clear mnemonics. Alert on any occurrence (DAD failures should not happen in normal operation). Investigate immediately if count > 5/hour on a single VLAN.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Syslog from Layer 3 network devices forwarded to Splunk. DAD failure syslog messages are generated at severity 4 (warning) on Cisco, so `logging trap warnings` or more verbose is required.
- For endpoint-level DAD detection: Splunk Universal Forwarder on Windows endpoints, forwarding System event log (Event ID 4227).
- Understanding that DAD is mandatory in IPv6 — every device performs DAD before using any IPv6 address (link-local and global). Unlike IPv4 where ARP duplicate detection is optional, IPv6 DAD is always on (unless explicitly disabled with `ipv6 nd dad attempts 0`, which is strongly discouraged).

### Step 1 — Configure data collection
No new data collection is typically needed — DAD failure syslog messages are generated by the same syslog infrastructure used for other network monitoring UCs. Verify:
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6" earliest=-7d
| rex "(?<ipv6_event>%IPV6[^ ]+)"
| stats count by ipv6_event
| sort -count
```
This shows all IPv6-related syslog mnemonics. Look for `%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE` — if absent over 7 days, either DAD failures aren't occurring (good) or the syslog level is too restrictive (check `logging trap` level).

For Windows endpoints, ensure System event log is forwarded:
```
# inputs.conf on Windows UF
[WinEventLog://System]
index = windows
sourcetype = WinEventLog:System
disabled = false
```

### Step 2 — Create the search and alert

**Primary search — DAD failure detection and classification:**
```spl
(index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE" OR "%IPV6_ND-6-DUPLICATE" OR ("%SISF" "DAD"))
OR (index=network sourcetype="juniper:junos" "DAD_FAILED" OR "dad-failed")
OR (index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:System" EventCode=4227)
| eval platform=case(
    sourcetype="cisco:ios", "Cisco",
    sourcetype="juniper:junos", "Juniper",
    sourcetype="WinEventLog:System", "Windows",
    1==1, "Other")
| rex "(?i)(?:duplicate|DAD|conflict).*?(?:address|addr)[= ]+(?<dup_address>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| rex "(?i)(?:interface|intf|adapter)[= ]+(?<interface>\S+)"
| where isnotnull(dup_address)
| stats count as dad_failures dc(host) as affected_devices earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by dup_address, interface
| eval first_seen=strftime(first_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval last_seen=strftime(last_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval addr_type=case(
    match(dup_address, "^fe80:"), "Link-Local",
    1==1, "Global Unicast")
| eval severity=case(
    dad_failures > 20, "CRITICAL — probable DAD DoS attack (see UC-5.20.87)",
    dad_failures > 5, "HIGH — repeated failures, investigate immediately",
    dad_failures > 1, "MEDIUM — recurring conflict",
    1==1, "LOW — single occurrence")
| sort -dad_failures
```

**Understanding this SPL:**
- Combines Cisco, Juniper, and Windows DAD failure messages into a single view. Each platform uses different syslog formats but all log the duplicate address.
- `addr_type` distinguishes link-local DAD failures (common during initial interface activation — less severe) from global unicast DAD failures (more severe — affects application connectivity).
- The severity classification escalates with failure count: a single DAD failure is likely a transient conflict or misconfiguration; > 20 failures in the search window strongly suggests an active DAD DoS attack where an attacker responds to all DAD probes on the VLAN.

**Real-time alert — any DAD failure:**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE" OR "%IPV6_ND-6-DUPLICATE"
```
Trigger: any result. DAD failures should not happen in normal operation. Any occurrence warrants investigation.

**Alert — DAD DoS attack pattern:**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE" OR "%IPV6_ND-6-DUPLICATE" earliest=-1h
| rex "(?i)interface\s+(?<interface>\S+)"
| stats dc(dup_address) as unique_addrs count as total_failures by host, interface
| where unique_addrs > 5 OR total_failures > 10
```
Trigger: > 5 unique addresses failing DAD on a single interface in 1 hour. This pattern — many different addresses all failing DAD — is characteristic of a DAD DoS attack where an attacker on the same VLAN responds to every DAD probe with a Neighbor Advertisement, preventing any host from acquiring an address.

### Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Test DAD detection:** On a test VLAN, configure two devices with the same static IPv6 address. The second device should generate a DAD failure syslog message. Verify it appears in Splunk within 1 minute.

(b) **Verify message parsing:** Check `| rex` field extraction on a sample event: `index=network "%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE" | head 1 | table _raw, dup_address, interface, host`. All fields should be populated.

(c) **Baseline confirmation:** In a healthy network, `index=network "%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE" earliest=-30d | stats count` should return 0 or very low numbers. If it returns hundreds, you have an ongoing address conflict problem.

(d) **Correlate with DHCPv6 DECLINE:** DAD failures for DHCPv6-assigned addresses should have a corresponding DHCPv6 DECLINE message (UC-5.20.10). Check: does the duplicate address appear in DHCPv6 logs as a DECLINE? If yes, the conflict is in the DHCPv6 pool.

### Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard** (panel on "IPv6 — Address Management"):
- Row 1 — Single-value: DAD failures (24h), red if > 0. "Days since last DAD failure" counter (green if > 7).
- Row 2 — Table: duplicate address, interface, device, count, severity, first/last seen.
- Row 3 — Timechart: DAD failures over 30 days.

**Alerting:** Real-time alert on any DAD failure. Escalate to security if count > 10/hour on a single interface (potential attack).

**Runbook** (owner: Network Operations):
1. Single DAD failure for a global unicast address: identify both devices claiming the same address. One has a stale static assignment, or SLAAC generated the same IID on two devices (extremely unlikely but possible with EUI-64 on devices with duplicate MACs).
2. Single DAD failure for a link-local address: link-local DAD failures on fe80:: addresses are more concerning because link-local is derived from MAC. Two devices with the same MAC on the same VLAN will produce this. Check for: cloned VMs with duplicate MAC, physical NIC replacement that reused a MAC.
3. Multiple failures on one VLAN: check for DHCPv6 pool overlap with SLAAC range. If the DHCPv6 server assigns addresses from the same /64 that SLAAC uses, DAD conflicts are expected.
4. Many unique addresses failing DAD: DAD DoS attack. Engage security team. Mitigation: enable SISF/FHS Source Guard and Destination Guard on the access switch to block the attacker's NA responses. See UC-5.20.87 for detailed attack response.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting

- **DAD events in syslog but not in Splunk** — Check syslog severity filtering. `%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE` is severity 4 (warning). If `logging trap errors` is configured (severity 3), DAD warnings are filtered. Change to `logging trap warnings` or more verbose.

- **DAD failure for an address nobody configured** — SLAAC-assigned addresses use the interface's MAC to generate the IID (EUI-64) or random IID (privacy extensions). If two devices generated the same random IID, a DAD conflict occurs. This is statistically improbable (~1 in 2^62) but not impossible. The correct resolution is for one device to regenerate its address.

- **Persistent DAD failure on router virtual address** — VRRP/HSRP/GLBP virtual IPv6 addresses may fail DAD if another router on the VLAN claims the same virtual IP. Check FHRP configuration on all routers in the VLAN for duplicate virtual IPv6 addresses or group number conflicts.

- **Windows endpoint reports Event 4227 but no router syslog** — DAD is performed by the endpoint, not the router. The router may not see the DAD failure if the conflict is between two endpoints on the same VLAN (the NS/NA exchange doesn't involve the router). The endpoint event is the authoritative source in this case.

## SPL

```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6-4-DUPLICATE" OR "%IPV6_ND-6-DUPLICATE" OR ("%SISF" "DAD")
| rex "(?i)(?:duplicate|DAD).*?(?:address|addr)\s+(?<dup_address>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| rex "(?i)(?:interface|intf)\s+(?<interface>\S+)"
| stats count as dad_failures earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by host, interface, dup_address
| eval first_seen=strftime(first_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval last_seen=strftime(last_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval severity=case(
    count > 10, "CRITICAL — possible DAD DoS attack",
    count > 3, "HIGH — repeated DAD failures",
    1==1, "MEDIUM — isolated DAD failure")
| sort -dad_failures
```

## Visualization

(1) Single-value tile: total DAD failures in last 24h (red if > 0, green if 0). (2) Table: duplicate address, interface, router, failure count, first/last seen, severity. (3) Timechart: DAD failures over 7 days — any pattern (time of day, day of week) helps identify the cause. (4) Status grid by VLAN/interface: green (no failures) vs red (failures detected).

## Known False Positives

**VRRP/HSRP virtual address DAD on failover.** When a VRRP/HSRP failover occurs, the new active router performs DAD for the virtual IPv6 address. If the old active router hasn't fully released the address, a brief DAD conflict may be logged. This resolves within seconds and is transient.

**VM migration (vMotion/live migration).** When a virtual machine migrates between hosts, both the source and destination host may briefly have the same IPv6 address, triggering DAD on the destination. This is transient and resolves as the source releases the address.

**Duplicate static assignments.** Two devices manually configured with the same static IPv6 address will produce persistent DAD failures. This is a real conflict (not a false positive), but the root cause is human error, not an attack. Remediate by fixing one device's address.

## References

- [RFC 4862 — IPv6 Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (§5.4 — Duplicate Address Detection)](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4862)
- [RFC 9099 — Operational Security Considerations for IPv6 Networks (§2.1.5 — DAD security)](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9099)
- [RFC 3971 — SEcure Neighbor Discovery (SEND) — cryptographic DAD protection](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3971)
